summary:
The Neanderthals (Homo neanderthalensis) were a species or subspecies of archaic humans who inhabited Europe and western Asia for approximately 400,000 years before becoming extinct around 40,000–30,000 BCE. Their extinction overlaps with the arrival and spread of anatomically modern humans (Homo sapiens) in Europe, and the relationship between Neanderthals and modern humans — coexistence, competition, interbreeding, or some combination — has been one of the most intensively studied questions in paleoanthropology.

Genetic evidence, dramatically revealed through ancient DNA studies beginning in 2010 with the pioneering work of Svante Pääbo's team at the Max Planck Institute, confirmed that Neanderthals and modern humans interbred. All humans today outside of sub-Saharan Africa carry approximately 1–4% Neanderthal DNA, evidence of genetic exchange during the period when modern humans expanded out of Africa and encountered Neanderthal populations. Some Neanderthal genetic variants have been shown to confer advantages in immune function and adaptation to new environments, while others have been associated with certain disease susceptibilities.

The causes of Neanderthal extinction — whether from competition for resources with modern humans, demographic replacement, interbreeding leading to absorption rather than extinction per se, climate change, or disease — remain debated. Current evidence suggests that Neanderthals declined gradually over several thousand years rather than disappearing suddenly, and that by approximately 40,000–30,000 BCE their distinct lineage had ended. The last known Neanderthal populations, possibly isolated in pockets of Iberia and southern Europe, persisted until approximately 30,000–28,000 BCE before disappearing entirely.

While the Neanderthals were gone well before the period of this era (10,000–8,000 BCE), they left a genetic and cultural legacy in modern humans that continues to be explored. Their sophisticated manufacture of Mousterian stone tools, use of fire, deliberate burial of the dead, production of personal ornaments and possibly pigments, and successful adaptation to the harsh Ice Age environments of Europe and western Asia mark them as cognitively and behaviorally complex beings whose disappearance represented a genuine loss of human diversity.
